Just now

Image created with GeminiThis blog series shares my thought process behind each NYT Connections puzzle I craft.
As a longtime fan of the game, I began making my own puzzles to reverse-engineer these brain-teasers and stretch my creativity. I’ve also noticed a parallel with solving business problems: both require analyzing a set of data and spotting patterns and relationships that aren’t immediately obvious.
Warning: the below contains answers for Play for Keeps by Uyi Uwadiae on Connections Plus
Wanted to try my hand at another connections like Piece It Together which was inspired by styles of custom puzzles I’ve seen on the NYT Connections subreddit. The basis of this one came from one of my categories in Pick me. I first started by listing as many words starting with each direction that I could think of, then tried to minimize overlap among the heads of each modifier.
🟨 Yellow: While 'Download' was the one word that kicked off my desire to craft this puzzle, I didn’t end up using 'Load' in this category because 'Front' had the least heads. This selection minimized overlap with the other categories besides 'Load' in Purple.
🟩 Green: As I thought 'Down' would be what jumps out first to solvers, I made 'Up' the next category—but also for stylistic reasons. 'Up' is tricky because it has a lot of potential options. 'Date' in Blue and 'Load' in Purple may cause issues but once players get this one, the rest will be easy.’Down'
🟦 Blue: This category is more straightforward and the heads for Back overlap minimally, if at all, in the puzzle.
🟪 Purple: While 'Front' has the least obvious overlaps which would potentially make this the easiest category. But, in my opinion, it’s the least obvious modifier that jumps out in the puzzle, which is why I made it Purple.
Find more of my Connections on Connections Plus!
You can play NYT Connections daily online or through the NYT Games app, available in the Apple App Store and Google Play.
